hypotheses.claim: The Currier A/B split reflects two distinct scribal hands encoding the same language using different glyph-frequency profiles, such that the top-10 word overlap between A and B sections is less than 40%, and the word-initial glyph distributions of A and B differ by Jensen-Shannon divergence > 0.15
evidence_for: Currier A contains 11,022 words vs B's 23,766 â€” a substantial split suggesting systematic difference
Current belief [0.456] supports a genuine linguistic or dialectal difference
High hapax ratio (70.1%) could reflect two vocabularies being conflated
evidence_against: If the same cipher is used with different keys, vocabulary overlap could still be low without reflecting language difference
A/B distinction may be purely codicological (different sections, not different scribes)
hypotheses.test: Compute Jensen-Shannon divergence between word-initial and word-final glyph frequency distributions of Currier A folios vs Currier B folios; also compute vocabulary overlap (shared word types / union of word types)
hypotheses.test_metric: JS divergence of glyph distributions and Jaccard similarity of vocabularies
hypotheses.test_threshold: JS divergence > 0.15 and Jaccard < 0.40 would support distinct encoding keys or languages; JS < 0.05 and Jaccard > 0.60 would support a single cipher
tests_remaining: Compute Jensen-Shannon divergence between word-initial and word-final glyph frequency distributions of Currier A folios vs Currier B folios; also compute vocabulary overlap (shared word types / union of word types)
hypotheses.reasoning: Distinguishing whether A/B is a cipher-key difference or a language difference is a high-leverage split: if JS is large but vocabulary overlap is moderate, a polyalphabetic key change is implicated; if both diverge strongly, distinct languages are implicated

hypotheses.claim: The manuscript uses a null-derived word-construction rule (e.g., a gallows-glyph prefix system) such that a small set of ~5 prefix glyphs accounts for > 60% of all word tokens, producing the observed Zipf exponent of ~0.89 via combinatorial explosion of a limited stem vocabulary
evidence_for: Word-initial glyphs are highly skewed: ['o','c','q','s','d'] dominate
Low Zipf exponent (0.8946) compared to natural language (~1.0â€“1.2) suggests constrained word formation
25 unique glyphs with average word length 5.03 is consistent with prefix+stem+suffix templating
evidence_against: Zipf R-squared of 0.9084 is a moderate fit, not exceptional â€” some natural languages also show exponents near 0.89
A prefix system alone would not explain the high hapax ratio of 70.1%
Glyph entropy 3.86 bits is close to Latin/Italian, suggesting phonemic rather than purely structural encoding
hypotheses.test: For each of the top-5 word-initial glyphs, compute what fraction of all word tokens begin with that glyph; then fit a prefix-stem bigram model and measure how much of the vocabulary is generated by combining those 5 initials with the top-50 word bodies
hypotheses.test_metric: Coverage fraction: (tokens with top-5 initial glyphs) / (total tokens); bigram generative coverage of vocabulary
hypotheses.test_threshold: Coverage > 0.60 and bigram model generating > 50% of unique word types would support a templated prefix system
tests_remaining: For each of the top-5 word-initial glyphs, compute what fraction of all word tokens begin with that glyph; then fit a prefix-stem bigram model and measure how much of the vocabulary is generated by combining those 5 initials with the top-50 word bodies
hypotheses.reasoning: If word formation is template-driven rather than phonemic, decipherment attempts based on phoneme-to-glyph mapping will systematically fail; confirming this would redirect effort toward structural rather than substitution models

hypotheses.claim: The text-only section (entropy 3.9016 bits, 7 folios) represents the closest approximation to natural language and encodes a different register or genre than the herbal/zodiac sections, such that its bigram entropy is statistically distinguishable (p < 0.05) from all other sections and its type-token ratio is highest among all sections
evidence_for: Text-only entropy (3.9016) is highest of all sections and closest to Latin (~4.0) and Italian (~3.95)
Absence of illustrations may mean less label-like, more prose-like text
Recipes section (3.8586) and text-only are the two highest-entropy sections, both likely prose
evidence_against: Only 7 folios â€” small sample may inflate entropy estimate
High hapax ratio across the whole manuscript makes type-token ratio comparisons noisy
hypotheses.test: Compute per-section bigram conditional entropy H(glyph_n | glyph_{n-1}) and type-token ratio (unique words / total words); run Kruskal-Wallis test across sections, then post-hoc pairwise Mann-Whitney U between text-only and each other section
hypotheses.test_metric: Bigram conditional entropy and type-token ratio per section
hypotheses.test_threshold: Text-only bigram entropy significantly higher (p < 0.05) than herbal and zodiac sections; text-only type-token ratio > 0.35 vs herbal < 0.25 would confirm distinct register
tests_remaining: Compute per-section bigram conditional entropy H(glyph_n | glyph_{n-1}) and type-token ratio (unique words / total words); run Kruskal-Wallis test across sections, then post-hoc pairwise Mann-Whitney U between text-only and each other section
hypotheses.reasoning: If text-only is linguistically distinct from illustrated sections, it should be the primary target for language identification; confirming this concentrates decipherment resources on the highest-signal section

hypotheses.claim: The high hapax rate (70.1%) is produced by a systematic nulls-insertion or verbose encoding scheme â€” specifically, that hapax words are morphologically related to non-hapax words by addition or substitution of a single terminal glyph, such that > 50% of hapax words have a Levenshtein distance of 1 from a high-frequency word
evidence_for: 70.1% hapax rate is anomalously high for any natural language or simple substitution of a natural language
Word-final glyphs ['y','n','l','r','o'] are highly constrained, suggesting terminal glyph variation is systematic
If a suffix-variation rule is applied to stems, it would produce many near-duplicate low-frequency forms
evidence_against: High hapax rate could also reflect a highly inflected language (e.g., Arabic, Hebrew, or a Slavic language) without any cipher
Levenshtein-1 clustering of hapaxes to high-frequency words would also occur in natural inflected languages
Current beliefs favor Latin/Italian [0.6], which are moderately inflected but not enough to explain 70% hapax rate
hypotheses.test: For each hapax word, compute the minimum Levenshtein distance to any word with frequency > 10; report the fraction of hapaxes within distance 1 and distance 2; compare this ratio to a Monte Carlo baseline of randomly shuffled word tokens
hypotheses.test_metric: Fraction of hapaxes with min-Levenshtein-distance-1 to a high-frequency word
hypotheses.test_threshold: Fraction > 0.50 (vs Monte Carlo baseline < 0.25) would support systematic suffix variation as a cipher mechanism; fraction near baseline would support natural morphological inflection
tests_remaining: For each hapax word, compute the minimum Levenshtein distance to any word with frequency > 10; report the fraction of hapaxes within distance 1 and distance 2; compare this ratio to a Monte Carlo baseline of randomly shuffled word tokens
hypotheses.reasoning: Distinguishing cipher-induced from morphology-induced hapaxes is critical: if hapaxes cluster near frequent words by edit distance, a verbose/null-insertion cipher is implicated and decipherment must account for variant forms

hypotheses.claim: The zodiac section (entropy 3.7149 bits, lowest of all sections) uses a label-oriented encoding in which most word tokens are proper nouns or short nominal forms, producing a word-length distribution significantly shorter than the herbal section (mean word length < 4.5 vs herbal > 5.0) and a lower bigram entropy reflecting repetitive naming conventions
evidence_for: Zodiac entropy (3.7149) is the lowest of all sections â€” lower entropy implies more repetition or constrained vocabulary
Zodiac illustrations depict figures with labels, consistent with a naming/labeling register
Astronomical section also has low entropy (3.7471), suggesting illustrative sections share a constrained label vocabulary
evidence_against: Low entropy could reflect a different cipher key rather than a different register
Small sample (1,297 words, 12 folios) limits statistical power
Word-length difference between sections may be confounded by section-specific glyph frequency profiles
hypotheses.test: Compute mean and variance of word length (in glyphs) per section; run a two-sample Kolmogorov-Smirnov test on word-length distributions between zodiac and herbal sections; also compute the fraction of zodiac words that appear in the top-200 most frequent words manuscript-wide
hypotheses.test_metric: Mean word length per section and KS test statistic for zodiac vs herbal word-length distributions; top-200 coverage fraction
hypotheses.test_threshold: KS p < 0.01 with zodiac mean < 4.5 and herbal mean > 5.0, and zodiac top-200 coverage > 0.70, would support a label register; overlapping distributions would suggest a cipher-level rather than register-level difference
tests_remaining: Compute mean and variance of word length (in glyphs) per section; run a two-sample Kolmogorov-Smirnov test on word-length distributions between zodiac and herbal sections; also compute the fraction of zodiac words that appear in the top-200 most frequent words manuscript-wide
hypotheses.reasoning: If zodiac is confirmed as a labeling register, its words are candidate proper nouns (star names, zodiac sign names) that could serve as cribs for decipherment via known medieval astronomical nomenclature
